planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Yuletide blooms in December–January in zone 9 — one of the latest-blooming sasanqua camellias — making fall planting ideal for root establishment before the bloom season.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Yuletide is perhaps the most distinctive and widely grown Camellia sasanqua cultivar for its brilliant red flowers with a contrasting boss of bright golden-yellow stamens — a vivid red-and-gold color combination that perfectly matches the traditional holiday color palette, earning the cultivar its name. While most sasanqua camellias bloom in October–November, Yuletide blooms in December–January in zone 9, providing genuine holiday-season color at the precise time when few other plants are in flower. The single, bright red flowers with the prominent golden stamens are smaller than japonica flowers but produced prolifically, covering the upright shrub with vivid color during the typically brown and gray winter garden season. The compact to medium-sized, upright habit grows 5–10 feet tall with dense, small, glossy, dark-green evergreen foliage. Yuletide tolerates more sun than most camellias — sasanqua types generally prefer more sun than japonica camellias — and performs well in 4–6 hours of direct sun with some afternoon protection. The late bloom season makes Yuletide less susceptible to freeze damage on open flowers than earlier-blooming camellias, though the flowers will be damaged by hard freezes below 28°F. Maintain consistent moisture and acidic soil pH for best performance.

common_name: Mine-no-Yuki Camellia (White Dove Camellia, Snow on the Mountain Camellia)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Mine-no-Yuki is a low, spreading cultivar well-suited to espalier training against walls and fences where its arching habit can be displayed horizontally.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Mine-no-Yuki (also sold as 'White Dove') is a classic Japanese camellia sasanqua cultivar producing beautiful, fragrant, large, white to cream-white, semi-double to double flowers from October through December in zone 9. The pure white to cream-white flowers with prominent golden-yellow stamens partially visible between the petals create an elegant, refined appearance that is particularly effective against dark evergreen foliage backgrounds, near entryways, or as cut flowers. Mine-no-Yuki has an unusually low-spreading, arching habit that sets it apart from the more upright growth of most sasanqua camellias — it typically spreads wider than it grows tall, which makes it outstanding for espalier training against fences and walls, for draping over retaining walls, and as a low screening or ground-cover plant in sloped situations. The mild, pleasant fragrance of the flowers is another distinctive characteristic — sasanqua camellias are generally more fragrant than japonica types, and Mine-no-Yuki is among the most pleasantly scented selections. The low, spreading habit and arching branches produce attractive natural mounding form without pruning. Provide moist, well-drained, acidic soil and maintain pine bark mulch at the root zone year-round.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Cleopatra is a vigorous, upright grower that can be maintained as a formal hedge or clipped into a topiary form — prune immediately after the bloom season ends to avoid removing next season's flower buds.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Cleopatra is a vigorous, upright Camellia sasanqua cultivar producing single to semi-double, rose-pink to deep-pink flowers with prominent golden-yellow stamens from October through December in zone 9. The medium-pink flower color is clear and saturated — a classic, feminine camellia pink that combines well with white-flowering companions, silver-foliage plants, and the dark evergreen foliage of camellias and hollies in the fall garden. Cleopatra is one of the more sun-tolerant sasanqua camellias, performing well in 4–6 hours of direct sun that would stress most japonica camellias, and it is more vigorous and faster-growing than many sasanqua selections. The upright, dense, somewhat narrow-columnar habit (6–12 feet tall, somewhat narrower than its height) makes it useful for screening and hedge applications where height is needed in a limited horizontal space. As with all sasanqua camellias, the fall flowers are mildly fragrant — pleasant but not overwhelming. Cleopatra was one of the foundational sasanqua cultivars widely planted across the Gulf South in the mid-twentieth century and remains a reliable, proven performer for zone 9 camellia gardens. Fertilize with azalea-camellia acidic fertilizer in spring and early fall.

common_name: Florida Prince Peach (Low-Chill Gulf South Peach)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in January–February in zone 9 in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), deep, fertile soil in full sun; Florida Prince is a true low-chill peach requiring only 150 chill hours, making it appropriate for the warmest parts of zone 9 and zone 10 where standard peaches fail.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), deep, fertile soil in full sun; Florida Prince is the standard low-chill peach cultivar for Gulf Coast zone 9–10 production — requiring only approximately 150 chill hours (hours below 45°F), compared to 800–1000 hours required by standard northern peach cultivars. This dramatically low chill requirement allows reliable peach production in coastal Louisiana, southern Texas, central Florida, and other warm winter areas where standard peach cultivars receive inadequate chilling to bloom and set fruit properly. Florida Prince produces medium-sized, round, yellow-fleshed, semi-freestone to clingstone fruit with a red blush on the sunny side — quality is good for fresh eating and suitable for canning and preserves, though not equal to the finest high-chill peach cultivars in flavor complexity. Fruit ripens in late April through May in zone 9 — very early in the season — which helps avoid much of the summer disease pressure that affects later cultivars. The tree is vigorous, productive, and well-adapted to the clay and sandy loam soils of the Gulf Coastal Plain. Thin fruit heavily (6–8 inches between remaining fruits) after natural June drop to develop full-sized fruits. Annual pruning to maintain an open-vase form is essential for air circulation and disease management in humid Gulf Coast conditions.

common_name: O'Neal Southern Highbush Blueberry (Low-Chill Highbush Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root shrubs in late winter in acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in full sun; O'Neal requires only 200–400 chill hours, making it one of the best highbush blueberries for zone 9; plant with another low-chill southern highbush cultivar like Legacy or Sunshine Blue for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in full sun; O'Neal is widely considered the finest-tasting southern highbush blueberry cultivar — a North Carolina State University release with remarkably low chill requirements (200–400 hours) that makes it appropriate for zone 9 and even warmer parts of zone 8, where northern highbush cultivars receive insufficient chilling. The large, medium-blue berries have exceptionally complex, aromatic, rich blueberry flavor — consistently rated among the best-tasting of all blueberry cultivars in taste trials by the North Carolina breeding program. The flavor profile is described as rich, aromatic, and complex with a good balance of sweetness and acidity. O'Neal is an early-season cultivar, ripening in April–May in zone 9, which helps avoid peak summer heat and provides early-season fruit at a premium time. The bush grows vigorously to 5–7 feet with attractive white urn-shaped flowers in late winter and outstanding fall color (orange to red) that makes it an ornamental landscape plant as well as a fruiting shrub. Plant with at least one other southern highbush cultivar (Legacy or Sunshine Blue) for cross-pollination, which dramatically increases berry size and total yield. Maintain strict soil pH between 4.5 and 5.5 — blueberries fail in soils above pH 6.0.
sources: North Carolina State University Small Fruit Research Program
